skill.name: face-swap
skill.description: Routes face and character swap requests to RunComfy CLI workflows for still images, video animation, motion transfer, and batch identity edits.
skill.summary: Run RunComfy face and character swaps for images and video.
skill.author: doany-ai

security_audit.summary: The static findings are mostly false positives from markdown backticks, model identifiers, and documentation URLs, not hidden executable code. Real risks remain because the skill authorizes RunComfy CLI use, sends identity media to external model APIs, stores API tokens locally, and enables dual-use face-swap workflows that require consent checks.

medium_findings.title: Dual-use Face-Swap Workflow Requires Consent Controls
medium_findings.description: The skill is designed to substitute one identity into images or video. It documents consent requirements and refusal guidance, but it does not enforce them before model execution.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The stated purpose is identity substitution, and the file explicitly says the skill itself does not gate inputs. This is a clear dual-use safety risk even though the author includes responsible-use warnings.

medium_findings.title: RunComfy CLI Commands Execute External Workflows
medium_findings.description: The skill permits Bash access for runcomfy commands and provides multiple runcomfy run examples. The scope is constrained, but execution can submit user assets to remote workflows and write outputs locally.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: There is direct evidence of allowed Bash use and concrete runcomfy commands. The risk is moderated because commands are limited to the runcomfy CLI rather than arbitrary shell snippets.

medium_findings.title: External Media Processing and Downloaded Outputs
medium_findings.description: The skill sends image, audio, and video URLs to RunComfy model APIs and downloads generated results. This can expose identity media and source assets to third-party infrastructure.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples use remote media URLs, and the how-it-works section states that the CLI posts to the model API and downloads results. This confirms third-party processing, though endpoints are documented as allowlisted.

medium_findings.title: API Token Stored in User Configuration Directory
medium_findings.description: The skill documents that runcomfy login writes an API token under the user configuration directory and recommends an environment variable for CI.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The token path is explicitly documented and uses restrictive permissions. This is legitimate CLI behavior, but it remains sensitive local credential storage.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified as Ruby Shell Execution
low_findings.description: Many external command findings are inline markdown code spans or fenced examples, not Ruby backtick execution. No Ruby source file or dynamic shell interpolation is present in the reviewed skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are markdown documentation containing inline code formatting and model identifiers. They do not execute as Ruby or shell code by themselves.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm Detections Are Unsupported
low_findings.description: The flagged lines contain the skill description, face-editing prose, tips, and exit code text. No evidence of MD5, SHA-1, DES, RC4, or cryptographic API usage was found in SKILL.md.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed file is markdown instructions and contains no cryptographic implementation on the cited lines. The static signal appears to be a keyword collision, not a confirmed vulnerability.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Documentation and Service Endpoints
low_findings.description: Hardcoded URLs point to RunComfy documentation, model catalog pages, example user CDN inputs, and related skill pages. They are expected for a RunComfy integration and are not hidden exfiltration endpoints.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible user-facing references or placeholder input locations. The security section also documents the intended outbound endpoint allowlist.

low_findings.title: Prompt Injection Discussion Is Defensive Guidance
low_findings.description: The file mentions indirect prompt injection only as a mitigation topic for untrusted reference assets. No instruction attempts to override evaluator, agent, or system behavior were found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited text warns agents to treat reference media as untrusted and restrict inputs to user-provided URLs. It is defensive guidance rather than a prompt injection attempt.

dangerous_patterns.title: Scoped Bash Permission for RunComfy CLI
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill declares Bash(runcomfy *) and instructs agents to run only runcomfy subcommands. This is a controlled command surface that still performs external operations.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Allowed tool scope and CLI-only limitation are explicit. The pattern is operationally necessary but should remain visible to users.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote Model API Submission Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The runcomfy run examples submit identity media, scene media, and prompts to hosted model endpoints. Generated outputs are downloaded into an output directory.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command examples and workflow explanation directly confirm remote submission and local downloads. This is expected for the service but relevant to privacy review.

dangerous_patterns.title: Credential Persistence Path
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill documents token persistence under the user configuration directory after login. File mode guidance reduces exposure but does not remove credential sensitivity.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The path and permissions are stated plainly. It is legitimate storage for a CLI token, but it is still a sensitive file location.

content.user_title: Create RunComfy Face Swaps for Images and Video
content.value_statement: Face and character swaps require choosing the right image or video model. This skill routes Claude, Codex, or Claude Code through RunComfy CLI workflows for stills, batches, and motion-driven video.
actual_capabilities: Selects a RunComfy route for still images, batch edits, character animation, or motion transfer.
Provides runcomfy CLI commands for Wan 2-2 Animate, Kling Motion Control, GPT Image 2 Edit, Nano Banana Edit, and Flux Kontext.
Explains required input fields such as identity images, scene images, audio URLs, and reference videos.
Gives prompt guidance for preserving pose, clothing, lighting, background, and identity consistency.
Documents consent, disclosure, output handling, exit codes, and token storage considerations.
limitations: Requires the RunComfy CLI, a RunComfy account, and valid authentication before use.
Does not enforce consent, identity rights, platform policy, or synthetic media disclosure by itself.
Relies on hosted RunComfy model endpoints and user-supplied media URLs.
Cannot run ComfyUI workflows that are outside the documented CLI model routes.

use_cases.title: Campaign Still Replacement
use_cases.description: Place an approved spokesperson face into a campaign image while preserving the original pose, clothing, lighting, and background.

use_cases.title: Identity-Consistent Product Gallery
use_cases.description: Apply the same approved identity across several SKU or lifestyle images with consistent aspect ratio and resolution settings.

use_cases.title: Motion-Based Character Video
use_cases.description: Transfer motion from a reference performance onto an approved target character for a stylized or photoreal short clip.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a Safe Route
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a consented image or video swap.
prompt_templates.prompt: I have consent to use these assets. Help me choose the right RunComfy face-swap route for a still image or video.

prompt_templates.title: Swap One Still Image
prompt_templates.scenario: Single-frame edit with a target scene and identity reference.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the approved identity reference for image 2 and the scene for image 1. Preserve pose, clothing, lighting, and background.

prompt_templates.title: Batch Identity Edit
prompt_templates.scenario: Several images need the same approved face or character.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Nano Banana Edit workflow for one identity reference and multiple scene images. Keep aspect ratio and resolution consistent.

prompt_templates.title: Motion-Controlled Video Swap
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced video workflow with a reference performance.
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a Kling Motion Control request using my reference performance video and approved target character image. Preserve motion and blocking.

output_examples.input: A user provides a consented portrait and one campaign scene image.
output_examples.output: The assistant selects GPT Image 2 Edit and returns a RunComfy workflow plan focused on preserving the original scene.

output_examples.input: A designer provides one approved identity image and several product lifestyle images.
output: The assistant chooses Nano Banana Edit.
It explains image ordering, consistency settings, and expected output handling.

output_examples.input: A video editor provides a reference performance and a target character image.
output_examples.output: The assistant selects Kling Motion Control and describes the required media URLs, consent checks, and output directory.

best_practices: Confirm consent and platform disclosure requirements before every face or character swap.
Use clear reference ordering and preservation instructions for still image edits.
Use only user-provided media URLs and review generated outputs before publication.
anti_patterns: Do not swap real people into sensitive, sexual, defamatory, or deceptive material.
Do not pipe unknown remote install scripts into a shell to install the CLI.
Do not use unrelated reference URLs or assets that the user did not explicitly provide.

faq.question: Does this skill perform the face swap locally?
faq.answer: No. It routes requests through the RunComfy CLI and hosted model endpoints.

faq.question: Can it handle both images and videos?
faq.answer: Yes. It documents routes for still images, batch image edits, animation, and motion-control video.

faq.question: Does it enforce consent automatically?
faq.answer: No. Users and agents must verify rights, consent, and disclosure requirements before running a workflow.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: What credentials are needed?
faq.answer: A RunComfy login or RUNCOMFY_TOKEN environment variable is needed for authenticated CLI use.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill is not malicious, but it runs external commands, sends identity media to remote services, and enables dual-use synthetic media.
